words.shortDefinition: An industry-standard protocol for authorization.
words.longDefinition: Allows a user to grant a third-party application limited access to their resources on another site (like 'Login with Google') without sharing their password. It focuses on delegated access.
words.techContext: We integrated OAuth2 so our users can sign up using their GitHub accounts instead of creating a new password.
words.exampleSentence: The OAuth2 flow requires the application to exchange an authorization code for an access token.

words.shortDefinition: JSON Web Token; a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties.
words.longDefinition: Consists of three parts: Header, Payload, and Signature. It is digitally signed, allowing the receiver to verify its authenticity and ensure the data hasn't been tampered with. Often used as a stateless session mechanism.
words.techContext: Our API uses JWTs to track authenticated users without needing to look up session data in the database for every request.
words.exampleSentence: I decrypted the JWT payload and found that the user's role was set to 'admin'.

words.shortDefinition: A simple identity layer on top of the OAuth 2.0 protocol.
words.longDefinition: While OAuth2 is for authorization (access), OIDC is for authentication (identity). It allows clients to verify the identity of the end-user based on the authentication performed by an Authorization Server.
words.techContext: We use OIDC to get a standardized 'ID Token' that contains the user's name and email address.
words.exampleSentence: OpenID Connect provided the user profile information we needed right in the authentication response.

words.shortDefinition: Adding random data to a password before hashing it.
words.longDefinition: A 'salt' is a unique, random string added to each password. This ensures that two users with the same password will have different hashes, preventing attackers from using precomputed 'rainbow tables' to crack them.
words.techContext: Even if two users use 'password123', their stored hashes look completely different thanks to unique salting.
words.exampleSentence: We use the bcrypt library, which handles salting and hashing automatically in one step.
words.etymology: Metaphor from cooking: adding 'salt' for extra flavor (uniqueness).

codeExample.code:
const salt = await bcrypt.genSalt(10);
const hash = await bcrypt.hash(password, salt);

words.shortDefinition: Multi-Factor Authentication; requiring more than one method of authentication to verify identity.
words.longDefinition: Combines two or more independent credentials: something you know (password), something you have (security key/phone), or something you are (biometrics). This significantly improves account security.
words.techContext: We mandated MFA for all employee accounts to protect against credential leaks.
words.exampleSentence: The login attempt was blocked because the user couldn't provide the MFA code from their authenticator app.

words.shortDefinition: An authentication scheme that allows a user to log in with a single ID to any of several related systems.
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as SSO. It allows users to authenticate once and gain access to multiple independent software systems without being prompted for credentials again.
words.techContext: We implemented SSO using Okta so our employees only need one set of credentials for Slack, Jira, and Zoom.
words.exampleSentence: SSO improves user experience and security by reducing the number of passwords a user has to remember.

words.shortDefinition: A security token that gives access to the 'bearer' of the token.
words.longDefinition: Anyone who possesses the token can use it to access resources. It does not require the user to prove they are the intended owner, which is why bearer tokens must be protected (usually over HTTPS).
words.techContext: Our API expects a Bearer token in the 'Authorization' header of every request.
words.exampleSentence: The request failed with a 401 Unauthorized error because the bearer token had expired.

words.shortDefinition: A long-lived token used to obtain new access tokens.
words.longDefinition: Access tokens are often short-lived (minutes) for security. When an access token expires, the client can use a refresh token to get a new one without forcing the user to log in again.
words.techContext: We store the refresh token in a secure, HTTP-only cookie to allow the user to stay logged in across browser restarts.
words.exampleSentence: When the access token expired, the app automatically sent the refresh token to the server to get a new session.

words.shortDefinition: A mechanism in OAuth 2.0 to limit an application's access to a user's account.
words.longDefinition: Scopes define the specific permissions an application is requesting. For example, a third-party app might request the 'read:profile' scope but not the 'write:settings' scope.
words.techContext: The consent screen showed the user exactly which scopes our application was requesting from their Google account.
words.exampleSentence: We reduced the requested scopes to only the bare minimum to increase user trust during sign-up.

words.shortDefinition: Identity Provider; a system that creates, maintains, and manages identity information.
words.longDefinition: The service that actually authenticates the user. In a 'Login with Google' flow, Google is the IdP. It provides authentication services to other applications (Service Providers).
words.techContext: Our corporate IdP is Azure Active Directory, which manages all employee logins.
words.exampleSentence: The Service Provider redirected the user to the IdP to verify their credentials.

words.shortDefinition: Role-Based Access Control; an approach to restricting system access to authorized users.
words.longDefinition: Permissions are assigned to 'roles' (e.g., Editor, Admin), and roles are assigned to users. This makes managing permissions easier as the team grows.
words.techContext: We implemented RBAC to ensure that only users with the 'Manager' role can approve expense reports.
words.exampleSentence: With RBAC, I just had to add the new hire to the 'Developer' group to give them all the permissions they needed.

words.shortDefinition: A web standard for passwordless authentication using public-key cryptography.
words.longDefinition: Allows websites to use built-in authenticators like TouchID, FaceID, or hardware keys (YubiKey) for secure, phishing-resistant logins. It is a core part of the FIDO2 project.
words.techContext: By implementing WebAuthn, we allowed our users to log in with a fingerprint instead of typing a password.
words.exampleSentence: The WebAuthn API handles the challenge-response protocol between the browser and the hardware security module.

words.shortDefinition: A safer and easier replacement for passwords.
words.longDefinition: Built on WebAuthn, passkeys are a type of digital credential that is stored on your device and synced across your other devices (via iCloud or Google Account). They are immune to phishing and data breaches.
words.techContext: We updated our auth flow to suggest creating a passkey after the user's first successful login.
words.exampleSentence: Passkeys eliminate the need for users to remember complex passwords or use MFA codes.

words.shortDefinition: Security Assertion Markup Language; an XML-based standard for exchanging authentication data.
words.longDefinition: Typically used in enterprise SSO environments. It allows an Identity Provider (IdP) to pass authentication 'assertions' to a Service Provider (SP). It is the older, XML-based relative of OIDC.
words.techContext: The enterprise client required us to support SAML 2.0 so their employees could use their existing corporate login.
words.exampleSentence: The SAML assertion contained the user's group memberships, which we used to set their permissions in our app.
words.etymology: Acronym for Security Assertion Markup Language.

words.shortDefinition: Attribute-Based Access Control; a fine-grained access control model.
words.longDefinition: Permissions are granted based on attributes of the user, the resource, and the environment (e.g., 'Allow managers to view files if they are in the office during business hours'). It is more flexible than RBAC.
words.techContext: We switched from RBAC to ABAC because we needed to restrict access to documents based on the user's specific department and the project's status.
words.exampleSentence: ABAC allowed us to create a policy that 'Only users with clear security clearance can access documents marked as SECRET'.

words.shortDefinition: An attack where the attacker sets a user's session ID to a known value.
words.longDefinition: The attacker tricks the user into using a specific session ID (e.g., via a link). If the application doesn't issue a *new* session ID after login, the attacker can use the known ID to hijack the user's account.
words.techContext: To prevent session fixation, our framework automatically regenerates the session ID every time a user logs in.
words.exampleSentence: We fixed a session fixation vulnerability by ensuring that the session cookie is reset upon successful authentication.

words.shortDefinition: A type of cyberattack where stolen account credentials are used to gain unauthorized access to other accounts.
words.longDefinition: Automated scripts use large lists of usernames and passwords leaked from other sites to attempt logins on many different platforms, banking on the fact that users reuse passwords.
words.techContext: We noticed a spike in failed login attempts, which looked like a credential stuffing attack using a leaked database from a different company.
words.exampleSentence: MFA is the best defense against credential stuffing because the stolen password alone isn't enough to gain access.

words.shortDefinition: An HTTP authentication scheme that involves security tokens called bearer tokens.
words.longDefinition: The client sends a token in the Authorization header. The name 'Bearer' implies that 'the bearer of this token' is authorized to access the resource.
words.techContext: Our mobile app uses Bearer Auth with JWTs to communicate with our backend services.
words.exampleSentence: The API returned a 403 Forbidden because although the Bearer Auth was valid, the user didn't have the necessary permissions.

words.shortDefinition: The most secure OAuth 2.0 flow for web and mobile apps.
words.longDefinition: The user authenticates with the IdP, which returns a one-time code to the client. The client then exchanges this code for an access token on the server side, ensuring the token is never exposed to the browser.
words.techContext: We use the Authorization Code Grant with PKCE to ensure the highest level of security for our React application.
words.exampleSentence: The authorization code grant flow prevents the access token from being intercepted in the browser's URL history.
words.etymology: Descriptive of the OAuth 2.0 process flow.

words.shortDefinition: Proof Key for Code Exchange; an extension to OAuth 2.0 for public clients.
words.longDefinition: Used to prevent authorization code injection attacks. The client creates a secret (code verifier) and sends its hash (code challenge). The server verifies the secret during the token exchange.
words.techContext: Modern best practices require using PKCE for all OAuth2 flows, including those for server-side applications.
words.exampleSentence: By adding PKCE to our mobile app's login flow, we protected our users from authorization code interception.
words.etymology: Acronym for Proof Key for Code Exchange.

words.shortDefinition: A JSON Web Token (JWT) that contains information about the authenticated user.
words.longDefinition: Part of the OIDC standard. Unlike an Access Token (which is for the API), the ID Token is for the client application to know who the user is (name, email, profile picture).
words.techContext: We use the ID Token to display the user's name in the header immediately after they log in.
words.exampleSentence: The app validated the signature of the ID token to ensure the profile information came directly from our trusted provider.

words.shortDefinition: A credential that can be used by an application to access an API.
words.longDefinition: It informs the API that the bearer of the token has been authorized to access the API and perform specific actions specified by the scopes that were granted during authorization.
words.techContext: Our access tokens have a short expiration of 15 minutes to minimize the impact if one is leaked.
words.exampleSentence: The mobile app sends the access token in every fetch request to the backend API.

words.shortDefinition: An OAuth 2.0 grant type for machine-to-machine (M2M) communication.
words.longDefinition: Used when an application needs to access its own resources or perform actions without a specific user involved (e.g., a background worker or a CLI tool).
words.techContext: Our microservices use the Client Credentials grant to authenticate with each other over the internal network.
words.exampleSentence: I had to create a new client secret to use the Client Credentials flow for our automated reporting script.

words.shortDefinition: The winner of the Password Hashing Competition (PHC) and a modern standard for secure hashing.
words.longDefinition: Designed to be highly resistant to GPU and ASIC-based brute-force attacks. It allows developers to tune memory usage and time to ensure that hashing is slow enough to be secure but fast enough for a good user experience.
words.techContext: We migrated our database from SHA-256 to Argon2id to provide state-of-the-art protection for our users' passwords.
words.exampleSentence: Argon2 is considered more secure than older algorithms like bcrypt or scrypt because of its superior memory-hard design.

words.shortDefinition: Linking a person's electronic identity across multiple distinct identity management systems.
words.longDefinition: Allows a user to use one set of credentials to access applications across different companies or domains. This is the underlying principle that makes 'Login with Google' or enterprise SSO work.
words.techContext: Federation simplifies user management by delegating authentication to a trusted external partner.
words.exampleSentence: Our application uses identity federation to allow users from our partner companies to log in using their own corporate credentials.
